findings.topic: KV cache is the dominant dynamic memory object and is manageable as paged virtual memory (vLLM PagedAttention)
findings.summary: Serving a 13B-parameter LLM on an NVIDIA A100 40GB: ~65% of GPU memory (26GB) is static weights, close to 30% is KV cache that grows/shrinks per request. For OPT-13B, the KV cache of a SINGLE token is 800KB (2 vectors x 5120 hidden x 40 layers x 2 bytes FP16), so a 2048-token sequence demands ~1.6GB. Existing contiguous-allocation systems waste most of this: measured actual token-state utilization of KV memory is only 20.4% (Orca Max), 26.8% (Orca Pow2), 38.2% (Orca Oracle) due to reservation + internal + external fragmentation, vs 96.3% for vLLM. PagedAttention divides KV cache into fixed-size blocks mapped via per-request block tables: 'one can think of blocks as pages, tokens as bytes, and requests as processes.' Result: 2-4x throughput at equal latency vs FasterTransformer/Orca, with gains growing for longer sequences and larger models. Copy-on-write at block granularity (ref-counted physical blocks) gives 6.1-9.8% memory saving on parallel sampling and 37.6-55.2% on beam search (Alpaca); 16.2-30.5% and 44.3-66.3% respectively on ShareGPT.
findings.designImplication: The agent-OS kernel should ship a default kernel-level 'context memory manager' that treats KV blocks exactly like page frames: per-agent logical-to-physical block tables, ref-counted copy-on-write for forked agent branches (multi-agent same-session forks map directly onto parallel-sampling sharing), and a default block allocator instead of contiguous per-context reservations. Context windows become an mmap-like kernel object, not an engine implementation detail. The 20-38% utilization number is the justification for making this a default kernel service rather than an optional userspace library: naive allocation wastes 3-5x of the scarcest resource (GPU HBM).
sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.06180 (SOSP '23, Kwon et al., read locally via pdftotext)

findings.topic: LLM-specific paging semantics: all-or-nothing eviction, gang scheduling, and recomputation as a page-fault handler (vLLM)
findings.summary: vLLM departs from classic VM in three documented ways: (1) all-or-nothing eviction — since all blocks of a sequence are accessed together every step, it evicts either all or none of a sequence's blocks; (2) gang scheduling — multiple sequences in one request (e.g., beam candidates) are 'gang-scheduled' and preempted/rescheduled together because of shared blocks; (3) two recovery paths for preempted sequences: swapping (copy evicted blocks to CPU RAM; swap space is bounded by the GPU KV pool size) and recomputation (regenerate KV from the token text in a single prompt-phase iteration, which 'can be significantly lower than the original latency' because decode-generated tokens are concatenated with the prompt and prefilled in one pass).
findings.designImplication: Kernel preemption of an agent's inference must operate at sequence-group granularity, not block granularity — the residency unit is 'an agent turn's working set'. Crucially, unlike classic VM, the durable state is the TOKEN TEXT, and physical KV is a reconstructible cache: the kernel can always 'page in' by recompute instead of by storage read. A greenfield kernel should expose both recovery policies (swap-to-host vs recompute-from-tokens) as a per-QoS-class policy knob, and its checkpointing/migration story for agents can serialize tokens (KB) rather than KV tensors (GB) — this is the key asymmetry an agent-native kernel can exploit that a generic OS cannot.

findings.topic: Cross-request prefix sharing via a radix tree over token sequences (SGLang RadixAttention)
findings.summary: SGLang keeps the KV cache of finished requests in an LRU cache organized as a radix tree mapping token sequences to KV tensors stored 'in a non-contiguous, paged layout, where the size of each page is equivalent to one token.' Eviction is leaf-first LRU with per-node reference counters (a node is evictable only at refcount 0); cached tokens and running requests share one memory pool, so 'when enough waiting requests run, the system will evict all cached tokens in favor of a larger batch size.' Cache hit rate is defined as cached-prompt-tokens / prompt-tokens; across benchmarks it ranges 50%-99%. Overall SGLang reaches up to 6.4x higher throughput and up to 3.7x lower latency vs state-of-the-art systems on agent control, few-shot, JSON decoding, RAG, and multi-turn chat workloads. The frontend sends fork prefixes ahead of payloads ('Frontend Hint') so the runtime inserts the shared prefix correctly.
findings.designImplication: The kernel's default context store should be a system-wide radix tree of token prefixes — the agent-OS analog of the page cache plus shared read-only segments. System prompts, tool definitions, OS-provided memory tiers, and skill documents become shared mapped segments: every agent that 'links against' the same prefix gets a free cache hit. Agent-spawn (fork) should be a first-class syscall that passes the shared prefix as a structural hint to the scheduler, exactly as SGLang's fork primitive does. Cache-vs-batch memory arbitration (one pool, batch wins) is a sensible kernel default policy.

findings.topic: Cache-aware scheduling is provably optimal as DFS over the prefix tree, but trades off fairness (SGLang)
findings.summary: SGLang sorts waiting requests by matched-prefix length (longest-shared-prefix-first) instead of FCFS, because FCFS switching between unrelated requests 'can lead to cache thrashing and a low hit rate.' Theorem 3.1: optimal cache hit rate is achieved by visiting the request radix tree in depth-first-search order, given cache size >= max request length; longest-shared-prefix-first is equivalent to DFS order. Measured: cache-aware scheduling reaches 96% of the optimal hit rate on average across benchmarks. Explicit caveat: 'greedy cache-aware scheduling can achieve high throughput, it can lead to starvation,' with fair-scheduling integration left as future work.
findings.designImplication: The kernel inference scheduler should be cache-topology-aware by default: runnable agent steps are nodes in the global prefix tree, and the dispatcher prefers DFS order within a QoS class. But because pure locality scheduling starves cold-prefix agents, the kernel needs aging/fairness as a first-class mechanism layered on top (e.g., deadline or virtual-runtime boosting), not an afterthought — this is precisely the CFS-vs-locality tension and it must be in the scheduler spec from day one, with per-QoS-class tolerance for batch re-ordering ('in more latency-sensitive settings we may still be able to tolerate limited batch re-ordering').

findings.topic: Request-level APIs destroy application knowledge; expose inference DAGs with per-output performance objectives (Parrot, OSDI 2024)
findings.summary: Parrot's diagnosis: agents issue many dependent LLM requests through 'the over-simplified request-level API,' so services 'blindly optimize individual LLM requests' — chatty client-service round trips alone slow end-to-end latency by over 2x, and per-request latency optimization misfires (map tasks want throughput batching, the reduce task wants latency). Parrot's Semantic Variable annotates prompt inputs/outputs, letting the service build a per-session DAG of requests, run dataflow analysis (GetProducer/GetConsumers), deduce per-request scheduling preference from the end-to-end objective annotated on final outputs (latency or throughput, extensible to per-token latency and TTFT), detect prefix commonality via PrefixHash at Semantic Variable boundaries, and execute dependent requests server-side through message queues without client round trips. Results: up to 11.7x speedup (multi-agent code review-and-revise on A100/LLaMA-13B) or 12x higher throughput; chain summarization latency cut 1.38-1.88x; a fused FlashAttention+PagedAttention kernel for shared prefixes gives 1.58-1.84x over vLLM.
findings.designImplication: This is the strongest argument that the agent-OS syscall interface for inference must NOT be 'one prompt in, one completion out.' The kernel should accept a graph of inference calls with typed dataflow edges (futures/promises over semantic outputs) and a performance objective attached to terminal outputs only; the kernel then derives per-node scheduling class by DAG analysis. Inter-step data should flow through kernel-managed channels (the agent never round-trips intermediate text through its own loop unless it wants to inspect it). For a greenfield design this is a kernel-level abstraction (like io_uring submission graphs), with the LLM engines as backends under it.

findings.topic: KV cache tiering across GPU/DRAM/SSD with SLO-driven scheduling and admission control (Mooncake, Moonshot AI / Kimi)
findings.summary: Mooncake disaggregates prefill from decode clusters and pools 'the CPU, DRAM, SSD, and RDMA resources of the GPU cluster to implement a disaggregated cache of KVCache.' KV is stored as paged blocks in CPU memory, each block keyed by a hash of itself plus its prefix for dedup; eviction via LRU/LFU/LengthAwareCache; 'the hottest blocks should be replicated to multiple nodes to avoid fetching congestion, while the coldest ones should be swapped out.' A global scheduler (Conductor) picks prefill+decode instance pairs balancing KV reuse against TTFT (time-to-first-token) and TBT (time-between-tokens) SLOs — waiting for KV on lower-tier storage may violate TTFT, so reuse is conditional. Under overload it applies prediction-based early rejection rather than admitting all requests. Numbers: up to 525% throughput increase in simulated long-context scenarios under SLOs; 75% more requests served under real workloads; in their trace, growing cache capacity from 1,000 to 50,000 blocks lifts hit ratio from 30% to 50% with diminishing returns after.
findings.designImplication: The OS's promised 'default persistent tiered memory structure for agents' has a direct hardware analog: a default KV/context tier hierarchy (GPU HBM -> host DRAM -> NVMe -> recompute-from-tokens) with prefix-hash-addressed blocks, hot-block replication, and pluggable eviction. Two kernel-policy lessons: (1) tier reads must be SLO-gated — fetching a cached prefix from SSD is only worth it if it still beats recompute within the agent's TTFT class; (2) admission control / early rejection is a kernel responsibility: an agent OS under token-pressure should reject or defer new agent steps early (predictively) rather than thrash all running agents.

findings.topic: Remote prompt caches are priced, leased memory: Anthropic TTLs, multipliers, breakpoints, and minimums
findings.summary: Anthropic prompt caching: default cache lifetime 5 minutes, 'refreshed for no additional cost each time the cached content is used'; optional 1-hour TTL. Pricing multipliers: 5-min cache WRITE = 1.25x base input price, 1-hour write = 2x, cache READ = 0.1x (e.g., Opus 4.8: $5/MTok base, $6.25 5m-write, $10 1h-write, $0.50 read; Sonnet 4.x: $3 / $3.75 / $6 / $0.30; Haiku 4.5: $1 / $1.25 / $2 / $0.10). Caching covers tools -> system -> messages in that order up to a cache_control breakpoint; the prefix hash is cumulative, so any change at or before the breakpoint invalidates it. Up to 4 explicit breakpoints; cache-read lookback is limited to 20 blocks per breakpoint; minimum cacheable prompt: 1,024 tokens (Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.x), 4,096 (Opus 4.5-4.7, Haiku 4.5), 2,048 (Haiku 3.5) — shorter prompts silently uncached. An 'automatic caching' mode moves the breakpoint to the last cacheable block each turn.
findings.designImplication: For remote backends the kernel cannot manage KV pages — it manages LEASES. The kernel's remote-backend driver should model each cached prefix as a lease object {prefix-hash, TTL, renewal-cost=0.1x-read, write-cost=1.25x/2x} and run a lease-renewal scheduler: keep hot agent contexts alive with sub-TTL touches, choose 5m-vs-1h TTL by predicted re-use interval (break-even arithmetic the kernel can do per agent), and enforce stable->volatile context layout (the OS's tiered agent memory must serialize its stable tiers first so they land before the breakpoint). Breakpoint placement, the 20-block lookback, and per-model minimums are exactly the kind of provider quirk that belongs in a pluggable backend driver beneath a uniform kernel 'context lease' API — not in agent code.

findings.topic: OpenAI caching contrasts: automatic, free, affinity-routed, with 24h offload tier — but no rate-limit relief
findings.summary: OpenAI Prompt Caching is automatic for prompts >= 1024 tokens, no code changes, no extra fee for writes; claims latency reduction up to 80% and input-cost reduction up to 90%. Mechanics: requests are routed to machines 'based on a hash of the initial prefix of the prompt' (typically first 256 tokens); a prompt_cache_key parameter combines with the prefix hash to influence routing; if one prefix+key combination exceeds ~15 requests/minute, overflow spills to other machines and reduces hit rates. In-memory retention: 5-10 minutes of inactivity, max ~1 hour off-peak. Extended retention (gpt-5.x family, gpt-4.1): up to 24 hours, implemented 'by offloading the key/value tensors to GPU-local storage when memory is full' (only KV tensors persisted, not prompt text; same price as in-memory). Critically, FAQ: 'Do cached prompts contribute to TPM rate limits? Yes, as caching does not affect rate limits.'
findings.designImplication: Two portable kernel concepts emerge: (1) cache-affinity routing is scheduler business — the kernel should generate and manage cache-affinity keys per agent/session (like CPU affinity masks) and shard fan-out workloads so no prefix+key lane exceeds the provider's ~15 RPM overflow threshold; (2) provider cache semantics diverge materially (OpenAI: free writes, automatic, counts against TPM; Anthropic: paid writes, explicit breakpoints, reads exempt from ITPM), so the kernel's backend-driver interface needs capability descriptors (ttl_range, write_cost, read_cost, counts_against_quota, affinity_hint_supported, min_cacheable_tokens) that the context scheduler queries — hardcoding either provider's model would be wrong. OpenAI's own 24h KV-offload tier validates KV tiering as the industry-wide direction.

findings.topic: Provider rate limits are hierarchical token-bucket quota systems with separate input/output token meters (Anthropic)
findings.summary: Anthropic enforces per-organization, per-model-class limits in three dimensions: requests/min (RPM), input tokens/min (ITPM), output tokens/min (OTPM), using 'the token bucket algorithm... capacity is continuously replenished up to your maximum limit.' Tier examples: Tier 1 Sonnet 4.x = 50 RPM / 30,000 ITPM / 8,000 OTPM; Tier 4 Opus 4.x = 4,000 RPM / 10,000,000 ITPM / 800,000 OTPM. ITPM is estimated at request start and adjusted during the request; OTPM counts actual generated tokens and max_tokens does not factor in. Limits are per-model so different models can be saturated simultaneously. Exceeding returns HTTP 429 with retry-after, plus live telemetry headers (anthropic-ratelimit-{requests,input-tokens,output-tokens}-{limit,remaining,reset}). Organizations can carve workspaces with lower sub-limits ('Workspace limits are set per limiter type'; org-wide limits always apply even if workspace limits oversubscribe). Spend limits stack on top: $500/month at Tier 1 up to $200,000 at Tier 4. Batch API has separate quotas (e.g., Tier 1: 50 RPM, 100,000 batch requests in queue).
findings.designImplication: This is a ready-made template for the kernel's token-budget controller: cgroup-style hierarchical token buckets per {backend, model, agent-group, agent}, with three independent meters (requests, input-tokens, output-tokens) and budget+period replenishment semantics — directly analogous to CPU bandwidth control (quota/period). The kernel should (a) mirror provider buckets locally using the ratelimit response headers as ground truth, scheduling preemptively instead of reacting to 429s; (b) support workspace-style oversubscribed sub-budgets for agent groups; (c) treat retry-after as a scheduler hint, parking the blocked agent without busy-waiting; (d) account spend (dollars) as a fourth meter, since monthly spend caps gate everything else.

findings.topic: Cache-aware quota accounting multiplies effective throughput: cache reads exempt from ITPM (Anthropic)
findings.summary: Anthropic's documented 'Cache-aware ITPM': for most models 'only uncached input tokens count towards your ITPM rate limits' — input_tokens and cache_creation_input_tokens count, cache_read_input_tokens do NOT (exception: Haiku 3.5 counts cache reads). Worked example from the docs: 'With a 2,000,000 ITPM limit and an 80% cache hit rate, you could effectively process 10,000,000 total input tokens per minute (2M uncached + 8M cached).' Total input is reconstructed as cache_read + cache_creation + input_tokens, and with a 200k-token cached document plus 50-token question, input_tokens reports just 50. OpenAI, by contrast, counts cached tokens against TPM.
findings.designImplication: Quota and cache placement are NOT independent kernel subsystems — on Anthropic-class backends, every point of cache hit rate is a direct multiplier on the org's token quota (80% hit = 5x effective ITPM). The kernel's context scheduler should therefore co-optimize: when an agent group nears its ITPM budget, the highest-leverage kernel action is restructuring context layouts for cache hits, not throttling. This also dictates accounting design: the kernel must meter uncached-input, cache-write, cache-read, and output tokens as four distinct flows per agent (matching usage-field granularity), because both cost and quota functions are linear combinations of them with backend-specific coefficients.

findings.topic: QoS classes for inference: latency-class vs throughput-class with distinct SLO metrics (synthesis of Parrot + Mooncake + provider tiers)
findings.summary: All sources converge on a two-axis QoS taxonomy. Parrot: map tasks 'should be batched more aggressively to increase the throughput' while the reduce task 'should be optimized for latency'; its get() annotation supports end-to-end latency, throughput, and is 'extensible to more criteria like per-token latency when streaming, and time-to-first-token'; throughput-preference propagates transitively to all producer requests in the DAG. Mooncake: SLOs are TTFT and TBT; its Conductor schedules against both while maximizing 'effective throughput' and early-rejects under overload. Providers expose matching service classes: Anthropic Priority Tier (committed-spend enhanced service, separate anthropic-priority-* headers), standard tier, and Batch API with its own queue-depth quotas (100k requests/batch) — i.e., a three-class remote QoS ladder; vLLM additionally shows latency-vs-throughput inversion at high load when systems become 'compute-bound rather than memory-bound.'
findings.designImplication: The kernel should define a small fixed set of inference QoS classes as ABI, e.g.: INTERACTIVE (TTFT+TBT SLO; maps to priority/standard remote tier, latency-batched local engine), PIPELINE (end-to-end-latency SLO over a DAG; intermediate nodes auto-derived as in Parrot), and BULK (throughput/cost-optimal; maps to remote Batch API or local throughput batching — the natural home for the OS's native self-improvement service, which is offline by nature). Each class carries its own admission policy (BULK is infinitely deferrable; INTERACTIVE is early-rejected under overload per Mooncake). Class assignment is per-output-objective with kernel-side derivation for interior DAG nodes, never per-request manual tuning.

findings.topic: Heterogeneous backend scheduling: local GPU and remote API are different resource types under one context-scheduler abstraction
findings.summary: The verified sources split cleanly into two resource models. Local inference (vLLM, SGLang, Mooncake): the schedulable resources are physical — GPU HBM KV blocks (800KB/token at 13B FP16), batch slots, prefill vs decode compute, host DRAM/SSD tiers — and the OS has full control of placement, eviction (LRU radix leaves, all-or-nothing sequence eviction), preemption (swap/recompute), and scheduling order (DFS-locality). Remote inference (Anthropic, OpenAI): the schedulable resources are contractual — token-bucket quotas (RPM/ITPM/OTPM), priced cache leases (0.1x reads, 1.25x/2x writes, 5m/1h/24h TTLs), affinity-routed cache placement (prompt_cache_key, ~15 RPM/lane), spend caps, and 429/retry-after backpressure. Nothing in either family exposes the other's knobs.
findings.designImplication: The greenfield kernel should define one neutral 'context scheduler' layer with two driver families beneath it: a local-engine driver class whose unit costs are bytes-of-HBM and GPU-seconds, and a remote-API driver class whose unit costs are dollars and quota-tokens. The shared upper-layer abstractions that both can implement: (1) prefix objects (content-hash-addressed, shareable, with per-backend residency state: GPU-resident / DRAM / SSD / remote-lease-active / cold), (2) token budgets (budget+period buckets in tokens and dollars), (3) QoS classes, (4) DAG submission. Placement of an agent step then becomes a cost-function decision across heterogeneous backends (e.g., a 150k-token context with an active Anthropic lease is 'cheaper' there than cold-prefilling a local GPU, until the lease expires). Default in-kernel: the scheduler and accounting; pluggable in userspace: each backend driver, mirroring the block-layer/driver split in Linux.

openQuestions: Kernel-vs-userspace boundary for local KV management: should the kernel own PagedAttention-style block tables directly (vLLM-in-kernel), or treat vLLM/SGLang as userspace engine servers and only schedule across them? The sources are all userspace systems; none addresses what a kernel-resident KV pager would gain or lose.

Fairness policy under cache-locality scheduling: SGLang explicitly leaves starvation-free cache-aware scheduling as future work ('it can lead to starvation... we leave its integration with other fair scheduling methods as future work'). What default aging/fairness mechanism should the kernel ship for multi-agent sessions sharing one prefix tree?

Cross-agent KV/prefix sharing security model: OpenAI isolates caches per organization; within a single agent-OS, when may two agents of different trust domains share a radix-tree prefix (timing side channels reveal whether another agent has 'seen' a prefix)? No fetched source addresses intra-host multi-tenant prefix-cache isolation.

Lease-renewal economics: at Anthropic pricing (read=0.1x refreshes the 5-min TTL free), what keep-alive cadence vs choosing 1h TTL (2x write) minimizes cost for a given agent idle-time distribution? The docs give the prices but no break-even guidance; the kernel needs this policy function.

Output-token budget reservation: Anthropic estimates ITPM at request start and counts OTPM only as generated (max_tokens not counted). How should the kernel reserve output budget for concurrent agents without over- or under-committing OTPM, given output length is unpredictable (the same unpredictability SGLang cites as breaking offline-optimal scheduling)?

Token-budget capability design: should budgets be transferable capabilities between agents (an orchestrator delegating ITPM slices to spawned workers, like Anthropic workspaces but nestable and revocable), and how do dollar-denominated spend caps compose with token-denominated buckets in one capability type?

Recompute-vs-fetch crossover on tiered storage: Mooncake states waiting on lower-tier KV may violate TTFT but gives no published crossover model (tokens/sec of prefill vs bytes/sec of tier bandwidth) the kernel could use as a default policy curve; this needs measurement per deployment.

DAG-aware remote execution: Parrot's gains assume the serving side executes the DAG; public APIs still expose request-level interfaces only. Can the kernel recover part of the 2x round-trip loss for remote backends purely client-side (speculative dispatch, streaming-pipelined dependent calls), or is this gain only available on self-hosted backends?
